facts.text: The blues originated in the Deep South of the United States in the late 19th century, rooted in African American spirituals, work songs, and field hollers.
The 12-bar blues is the most common chord progression in blues music, consisting of three four-bar phrases using the I, IV, and V chords.
Robert Johnson recorded only 29 songs in his lifetime, yet he is considered one of the most influential blues musicians of all time.
The term 'blues' likely derives from the phrase 'blue devils,' meaning melancholy or sadness, used in English since the 1600s.
W.C. Handy, known as the 'Father of the Blues,' published 'Memphis Blues' in 1912, one of the first blues compositions in sheet music form.
The Mississippi Delta, a region of northwestern Mississippi, is widely regarded as the birthplace of Delta blues.
Muddy Waters was born McKinley Morganfield in Issaquena County, Mississippi.
A 'blue note' is a note sung or played at a slightly lower pitch than standard for expressive purposes, typically the third, fifth, or seventh scale degree.
Chess Records, founded in Chicago in 1950, recorded landmark blues albums by Muddy Waters, Howlin' Wolf, and Chuck Berry.
B.B. King named all of his guitars 'Lucille' after a near-fatal incident at a dance hall fire in Twist, Arkansas, in 1949.
The Great Migration of the early 20th century saw millions of African Americans move from the rural South to northern cities, carrying the blues with them.
Juke joints were small, informal establishments in the rural South where African Americans gathered to listen to blues music, dance, and socialize.
Robert Johnson was born in Hazlehurst, Mississippi.
The slide guitar technique, using a glass or metal slide on the strings, is a signature sound in Delta blues.
Howlin' Wolf stood six feet three inches tall and weighed nearly 300 pounds, giving him an imposing stage presence to match his powerful voice.
The blues scale adds a flatted fifth to the minor pentatonic scale, giving it its characteristic tension and expressiveness.
Bessie Smith, known as the 'Empress of the Blues,' was the highest-paid Black entertainer of the 1920s.
Lead Belly, born Huddie William Ledbetter, was a folk and blues musician known for songs like 'Goodnight, Irene' and 'Midnight Special.'
The first blues recording ever made is often credited to Mamie Smith's 'Crazy Blues,' recorded in 1920.
Charley Patton is considered the 'Father of the Delta Blues' and was one of the earliest recorded Delta blues musicians.
B.B. King's signature vibrato technique involved rapidly shaking his left hand on the guitar string instead of bending it.
The crossroads legend holds that Robert Johnson sold his soul to the devil at a Mississippi crossroads in exchange for mastery of the guitar.
Stevie Ray Vaughan was born in Dallas, Texas.
Son House was a Delta blues pioneer who influenced both Robert Johnson and Muddy Waters.
Muddy Waters is often called the 'father of modern Chicago blues' for electrifying the Delta blues sound.
The harmonica, also called a blues harp, is one of the most essential instruments in blues music alongside the guitar.
Little Walter Jacobs revolutionized blues harmonica by amplifying it through a microphone and guitar amplifier.
Albert King played his Gibson Flying V guitar left-handed and upside down, without restringing it.
T-Bone Walker is credited with pioneering the electric blues guitar in the 1940s.
John Lee Hooker's boogie-based blues style featured a driving, hypnotic one-chord groove that became his trademark.
Etta James was born Jamesetta Hawkins in Los Angeles, California, in 1938.
The blues influenced virtually every major American music genre, including rock and roll, jazz, R&B, soul, and country.
Buddy Guy was born in Lettsworth, Louisiana.
The 'call and response' pattern, where a vocalist sings a phrase and an instrument answers, is a defining feature of blues music.
Sam Phillips founded Sun Records in Memphis in 1952, recording early blues, rockabilly, and rock and roll artists.
Elmore James was known as the 'King of the Slide Guitar' for his fierce bottleneck slide playing.
The Chicago blues style developed in the 1940s and 1950s as Delta blues musicians plugged in electric guitars and added full bands.
Skip James developed a unique minor-key blues style and an eerie falsetto singing voice.
Freddie King was known for his powerful, hard-driving guitar instrumentals like 'Hide Away' and 'San-Ho-Zay.'
The 'AAB' lyric form, where the first line is repeated and then answered by a third line, is the most common blues verse structure.
Lightnin' Hopkins was one of the most prolific blues recording artists, with hundreds of songs to his name.
The Mississippi Delta region stretches from Memphis, Tennessee, to Vicksburg, Mississippi, and is a flat, fertile alluvial plain.
B.B. King was born Riley B. King near Itta Bena, Mississippi.
Memphis Minnie was a pioneering female blues guitarist and singer who competed head-to-head with male blues musicians.
The first Grammy Award for Best Traditional Blues Album was given in 1983.
Tampa Red, known as the 'Guitar Wizard,' was a master of the slide guitar and an important early Chicago blues figure.
Big Mama Thornton recorded the original version of 'Hound Dog' in 1952, three years before Elvis Presley's version.
The resonator guitar, including the Dobro and National brands, was developed in the 1920s to produce a louder acoustic sound for blues musicians.
Junior Wells was a Chicago blues harmonica player and vocalist best known for 'Messin' with the Kid.'
The Piedmont blues style from the southeastern United States features a fingerpicking technique with an alternating bass pattern.
Willie Dixon was a prolific blues songwriter who penned classics like 'Hoochie Coochie Man,' 'I Just Want to Make Love to You,' and 'Spoonful.'
Texas blues is characterized by a smoother, more jazzy guitar style compared to the rawer Delta blues.
Ma Rainey, known as the 'Mother of the Blues,' was one of the earliest professional blues singers.
Koko Taylor, known as the 'Queen of the Blues,' was famous for her powerful rendition of 'Wang Dang Doodle.'
The Devil's music was a term used by many churchgoers in the early 20th century to describe the blues.
Acoustic blues typically features solo performers playing guitar and singing, often in an intimate, storytelling style.
Blind Lemon Jefferson was one of the first commercially successful solo blues recording artists in the 1920s.
The standard blues band lineup typically includes guitar, bass, drums, harmonica, and vocals.
Albert Collins was known as the 'Ice Man' for his stinging, trebly guitar tone and cold-themed song titles.
Howlin' Wolf was born Chester Arthur Burnett in White Station, Mississippi.
The blues was added to the Grammy Awards as its own category in 1983.
Pinetop Perkins was a legendary blues pianist who performed with Muddy Waters and was still performing past the age of 90.
The walking bass line, a steady quarter-note pattern moving stepwise through a chord progression, is a staple of blues bass playing.
Bobby 'Blue' Bland blended blues with soul and gospel to create a smooth, sophisticated sound.
Blind Willie McTell was a Piedmont and ragtime blues singer and twelve-string guitar player from Georgia.
The term 'boogie-woogie' describes a driving, up-tempo piano blues style that originated in the early 20th century.
Etta James recorded her signature song 'At Last' in 1960.
Taj Mahal has been a leading figure in preserving and expanding the acoustic blues tradition since the 1960s.
Muddy Waters' 1958 tour of England profoundly influenced the British blues movement and future rock musicians.
The Blues Brothers, Dan Aykroyd and John Belushi, brought mainstream attention to blues music with their 1980 film.
The Bo Diddley beat, a syncopated rhythm pattern, became a foundational groove in blues and rock and roll.
Otis Rush was a Chicago blues guitarist known for his left-handed, upside-down guitar playing and emotional vocals.
The Chitlin' Circuit was a network of performance venues across the United States that were safe and welcoming for African American musicians during segregation.
Guitar Slim is credited with one of the first uses of heavy distortion on an electric guitar in his 1953 hit 'The Things That I Used to Do.'
The Mississippi Blues Trail features historical markers across the state honoring blues musicians and landmarks.
Hound Dog Taylor was a raw, energetic Chicago slide guitarist known for his wild live performances.
Albert King was born Albert Nelson in Indianola, Mississippi.
The blues turnaround is a short musical phrase at the end of a verse that leads back to the beginning of the next verse.
Sonny Boy Williamson II, born Aleck Rice Miller, was a masterful blues harmonica player and one of the first blues musicians to have his own radio show.
The acoustic guitar, both steel-string and resonator, has been the primary instrument in blues music since its origins.
R.L. Burnside was a Mississippi hill country blues musician who blended raw blues with a hypnotic, trance-like groove.
Jimmy Reed was one of the most commercially successful blues musicians of the 1950s and 1960s, known for his relaxed, laid-back style.
The National steel-bodied resonator guitar became a favorite of blues musicians for its loud, metallic tone.
Eric Clapton's love of blues music, particularly the work of Robert Johnson and B.B. King, was a driving force behind the British blues boom.
Lonnie Johnson was a guitar virtuoso who bridged jazz and blues, influencing both genres in the 1920s and 1930s.
The Delta blues style is characterized by passionate, raw vocals and slide guitar, often performed solo.
Howlin' Wolf moved to Chicago in 1952 and signed with Chess Records, where he recorded many of his most famous songs.
Magic Sam was a Chicago blues guitarist whose West Side style influenced a generation of modern blues players.
Blind Willie Johnson's 'Dark Was the Night, Cold Was the Ground' was included on the Voyager Golden Record sent into space in 1977.
The shuffle rhythm, a swung triplet feel, is one of the most recognizable rhythmic patterns in blues music.
Blues clubs on Chicago's South Side, such as Theresa's Lounge and Pepper's Lounge, were legendary venues in the 1950s and 1960s.
Kokomo Arnold was a Delta blues bottle-neck slide guitarist known for his fast, virtuosic playing style.
The Gibson ES-335, a semi-hollow body electric guitar, has been a favorite of many blues players including B.B. King and Freddie King.
Johnny Winter was an albino Texas blues guitarist who became one of the highest-paid rock and blues artists of the early 1970s.
Bukka White was a Delta blues musician and cousin of B.B. King, known for powerful slide guitar and rail-road themed songs.
The 'mojo hand' is a charm from African American folk magic that appears frequently in blues lyrics.
John Lee Hooker was born near Clarksdale, Mississippi.
The Fender Stratocaster became an iconic blues guitar thanks to players like Buddy Guy and Stevie Ray Vaughan.
W.C. Handy first encountered the blues at a train station in Tutwiler, Mississippi, around 1903, when he heard a man playing slide guitar with a knife.
Big Bill Broonzy was a prolific blues musician who recorded over 300 songs and helped transition acoustic blues to the electric Chicago style.
The blue note is not simply a flatted note but can be any microtonal pitch bending that gives the blues its expressive, vocal quality.
Parchman Farm, the Mississippi State Penitentiary, was where many blues musicians like Bukka White and Son House served time and refined their music.
Stevie Ray Vaughan's debut album 'Texas Flood' was recorded in just three days.
Professor Longhair blended blues with New Orleans R&B, rumba, and boogie-woogie, creating a unique piano style.
The blues revival of the 1960s brought renewed interest in acoustic Delta blues among white folk music audiences.
Bonnie Raitt is known for her bottleneck slide guitar playing and her longtime championing of blues music.
Bessie Smith was born in Chattanooga, Tennessee.
Mississippi Fred McDowell was a hill country blues musician whose raw, powerful style influenced the Rolling Stones.
The pentatonic scale, containing five notes, forms the backbone of most blues melodies and guitar solos.
Luther Allison was a Chicago and Texas blues guitarist who gained a massive following in Europe in the 1980s and 1990s.
Sugar Blue is a blues harmonica player who played on the Rolling Stones' hit 'Miss You.'
The blues has its roots in the plantation work songs and spirituals of enslaved African Americans in the Mississippi Delta.
Robert Cray brought blues back to mainstream audiences in the 1980s with his smooth, soulful guitar playing and songwriting.
Maxwell Street in Chicago was an open-air market where blues musicians performed and launched their careers.
Sister Rosetta Tharpe, often called the 'Godmother of Rock and Roll,' blended gospel with blues and pioneered electric guitar distortion.
The 8-bar blues is a shorter variation of the standard 12-bar blues used in songs like 'Key to the Highway.'
Freddie King was born in Gilmer, Texas.
Lowell Fulson was a blues guitarist and songwriter who penned 'Reconsider Baby,' later recorded by Elvis Presley.
Muddy Waters' real name was McKinley Morganfield; he got the nickname 'Muddy' because he played in the muddy creek as a child.
Acoustic Delta blues was typically performed by a single artist on guitar, sometimes with a harmonica on a neck rack.
Robert Nighthawk was a Delta blues slide guitarist whose smooth, sustained tone influenced many later electric blues players.
The term 'the blues' first appeared in printed lyrics in 1908 in a song called 'I Got the Blues.'
Otis Spann is widely regarded as the greatest blues pianist in the postwar Chicago blues era.
Washboard Sam was a blues musician who literally played a washboard as a percussive instrument.
The juke joint derives its name from 'jook' or 'juke,' a word from Gullah Geechee culture meaning rowdy or disorderly.
Furry Lewis was a Memphis blues musician who was rediscovered during the 1960s folk blues revival.
Stevie Ray Vaughan died in a helicopter crash near East Troy, Wisconsin.
Buddy Guy's guitar playing influenced Jimi Hendrix, Eric Clapton, and many other rock guitarists.
The diddley bow, a one-stringed instrument made from wire nailed to a wall or board, was an early blues instrument played in the rural South.
The British blues boom of the 1960s saw bands like the Rolling Stones, the Yardbirds, and Fleetwood Mac draw heavily from American blues.
J.B. Lenoir was a Chicago blues musician known for his high-pitched vocals and socially conscious lyrics about poverty and racism.
Earl Hooker, cousin of John Lee Hooker, was considered one of the finest slide guitarists in blues history.
The Allman Brothers Band combined blues with rock and Southern influences, creating the Southern rock genre.
Lazy Lester was a Louisiana swamp blues harmonica player and vocalist who recorded for Excello Records.
The 16-bar blues is a less common blues form used in songs like 'Watermelon Man' by Herbie Hancock.
David 'Honeyboy' Edwards was the last surviving musician to have personally known and traveled with Robert Johnson.
W.C. Handy was born in Florence, Alabama.
Carey Bell was a Chicago blues harmonica player who carried on the tradition of Little Walter and Sonny Boy Williamson.
The blues harp, played in 'cross harp' or second position, allows harmonica players to bend notes and achieve the characteristic bluesy sound.
Mississippi John Hurt was a gentle fingerpicking acoustic blues and folk musician rediscovered during the 1960s revival.
Slim Harpo was a Louisiana swamp blues musician best known for 'I'm a King Bee' and 'Baby Scratch My Back.'
Keb' Mo' is a modern blues artist who blends traditional Delta blues with contemporary folk and R&B.
The first Blues Foundation 'Blues Music Award' ceremony was held in Memphis in 1980.
Champion Jack Dupree was a New Orleans blues pianist and singer who spent much of his later career in Europe.
The bottleneck slide was originally made from the neck of a glass bottle, broken or cut to fit over a guitarist's finger.
Peter Green, the founder of Fleetwood Mac, was one of the most revered British blues guitarists.
B.B. King performed at his first concert in Indianola, Mississippi, in 1947.
Clarence 'Gatemouth' Brown was a versatile Texas blues musician who played guitar, fiddle, harmonica, drums, and mandolin.
Muddy Waters' 'Rollin' Stone' inspired the name of both the Rolling Stones and Rolling Stone magazine.
The 'Devil's interval,' or tritone, appears frequently in blues music and was historically considered dissonant.
Sunnyland Slim was a veteran Chicago blues pianist who helped bring fellow musicians like Muddy Waters and Howlin' Wolf to Chess Records.
Eddie Boyd was a Chicago blues pianist and singer known for 'Five Long Years.'
Robert Johnson's recordings from 1936 and 1937 in San Antonio and Dallas were the only studio sessions he ever made.
Louisiana blues is a subgenre that blends the blues with zydeco, swamp pop, and Cajun musical influences.
Shemekia Copeland, daughter of blues guitarist Johnny Copeland, is one of the leading modern female blues vocalists.
The Gibson Les Paul has been a preferred guitar for many blues-rock players including Jimmy Page and Gary Moore.
Howlin' Wolf was born on June 10, 1910, in White Station, Mississippi.
The King Biscuit Time radio show, first broadcast in 1941, was the first regular radio program to feature live blues music.
John Mayall's Bluesbreakers served as a launching pad for British blues guitarists including Eric Clapton, Peter Green, and Mick Taylor.
Arthur 'Big Boy' Crudup wrote 'That's All Right,' which was Elvis Presley's first single and a pivotal record in rock and roll history.
The 'rhythm changes' progression in jazz was influenced by the blues' use of the I-IV-V chord movement.
Honeyboy Edwards received his nickname from his mother, who said he was sweet as honey as a baby.
Henry Thomas, known as 'Ragtime Texas,' was one of the earliest Texas blues musicians to be recorded, in the late 1920s.
The blues standard 'Sweet Home Chicago' is one of the most performed and recorded blues songs of all time.
Robert Lockwood Jr. was the only guitarist personally taught by Robert Johnson and went on to a long recording career.
The wah-wah pedal, which creates a voice-like effect, has been used by blues guitarists like Buddy Guy and Jimi Hendrix.
Etta James was inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame in 1993.
The Hill Country blues of northern Mississippi is characterized by a droning, trance-like groove with heavy emphasis on rhythm.
Homesick James Williamson was a Chicago blues slide guitarist who claimed to be the cousin of Elmore James.
ZZ Top's early music was heavily influenced by Texas and Chicago blues.
Willie Brown was a Delta blues musician and frequent playing partner of Charlie Patton and Son House.
The acoustic 12-string guitar produces a rich, full-bodied sound favored by blues musicians like Lead Belly and Blind Willie McTell.
Little Milton was a blues and soul singer-guitarist whose hit 'We're Gonna Make It' reached number one on the R&B chart in 1965.
Detroit blues developed a distinctive style influenced by the auto industry's working-class culture and musicians like John Lee Hooker.
Snooks Eaglin was a blind New Orleans blues and R&B guitarist known for his extraordinary versatility and repertoire.
The Mississippi Sheiks were an influential string band of the 1930s, best known for 'Sitting on Top of the World.'
Robert Johnson died at age 27 near Greenwood, Mississippi.
Tinsley Ellis is a modern blues-rock guitarist and singer from Atlanta, Georgia.
The resonator guitar was invented to be heard above big bands and noisy dance halls before electric amplification was available.
Chris Thomas King is a blues musician and actor who played the Robert Johnson-inspired character Tommy Johnson in the film 'O Brother, Where Art Thou?'
Matt 'Guitar' Murphy was a blues guitarist who gained fame through his appearance in 'The Blues Brothers' film.
The Paramount Records label, based in Grafton, Wisconsin, recorded many early Delta blues artists in the 1920s and 1930s.
Stevie Ray Vaughan's guitar 'Number One' was a battered 1963 Fender Stratocaster with a left-handed tremolo bar.
The 'riff' in blues music is a short, repeated musical phrase that forms the basis of a song's melody or groove.
Joe Louis Walker is a Bay Area blues musician who blends blues, gospel, and rock.
The West Side Chicago blues style, pioneered by Magic Sam and Otis Rush, emphasized soulful vocals and distorted electric guitar.
Albert King was left-handed and played a right-handed Gibson Flying V flipped upside down.
Cedric Burnside, grandson of R.L. Burnside, won the Grammy for Best Traditional Blues Album in 2022.
The earliest blues songs were not written down but passed along orally from musician to musician.
Billy Branch is a modern Chicago blues harmonica player and bandleader who has performed since the 1970s.
The cigar box guitar, a homemade instrument built from a cigar box, broom handle, and wire, was one of the earliest instruments used in blues.
Smokey Hogg was a Texas blues singer and guitarist popular in the late 1940s and early 1950s.
Lightnin' Hopkins once said he played the guitar like a conversation, telling stories with every note.
Canned Heat was a blues-rock band named after a 1928 blues song by Tommy Johnson about drinking Sterno.
The Mississippi Blues Commission has placed over 200 historical markers along the Mississippi Blues Trail.
Phillip Walker was a Louisiana-born, Texas-raised blues guitarist who later became prominent in the West Coast blues scene.
Lead Belly was born near Mooringsport, Louisiana.
Big Joe Turner was a Kansas City blues shouter whose hit 'Shake, Rattle and Roll' helped bridge blues and early rock and roll.
The Blues Foundation, headquartered in Memphis, Tennessee, is the preeminent organization dedicated to preserving blues heritage.
Son House's intense, impassioned singing style made him one of the most emotionally powerful performers in blues history.
Kenny Wayne Shepherd is a modern blues-rock guitarist from Shreveport, Louisiana, who had mainstream success in the 1990s.
The Kansas City blues style features a more swinging, jazz-influenced feel than the rawer Delta blues.
Dinah Washington, the 'Queen of the Blues,' was known for blending blues, jazz, and pop in her vocal performances.
Sippie Wallace was an early female blues singer from Houston who was active in the 1920s classic blues era.
Gary Clark Jr. is a modern blues-rock musician from Austin, Texas, who draws on traditional blues, rock, and hip-hop.
Blues lyrics frequently address themes of hardship, heartbreak, travel, poverty, and resilience.
Muddy Waters recorded 'I Can't Be Satisfied' and 'I Feel Like Going Home' for Aristocrat Records.
The Delta blues guitar style often uses open tunings, such as open D and open G, to facilitate slide playing.
Paul Butterfield, a white blues harmonica player from Chicago, helped bring electric blues to white audiences in the 1960s.
Clifton Chenier was the 'King of Zydeco,' blending Cajun accordion music with blues and R&B.
Jimmy Dawkins was a West Side Chicago blues guitarist known for his lean, intense playing style.
The blues influenced the development of gospel music, which in turn gave rise to soul music in the 1960s.
Mississippi Fred McDowell famously said, 'I do not play no rock and roll,' insisting his music was pure, authentic blues.
Tab Benoit is a Louisiana blues musician and conservationist who works to protect Gulf Coast wetlands.
The 'turnaround' lick at the end of a 12-bar blues verse is a signature element that signals the return to the top of the form.
Fenton Robinson was a blues guitarist and singer from Mississippi known for his jazz-tinged guitar work.
Charley Patton died near Indianola, Mississippi.
The electric bass guitar replaced the upright bass in most blues bands during the 1950s and 1960s.
Eric Clapton's 'Unplugged' album featured blues standards and revived mainstream interest in acoustic blues in 1992.
Corey Harris is a modern acoustic blues musician who has explored the connections between the blues and West African music.
Guitar Gabriel was a Piedmont blues musician from North Carolina who played in a country-blues fingerpicking style.
The trombone and trumpet are commonly used in larger blues ensemble settings, especially in jump blues and big-band blues.
Louisiana Red was a blues guitarist and singer who recorded over 50 albums during his long career.
The King Biscuit Blues Festival is held annually in Helena, Arkansas, and is one of the largest free outdoor blues festivals in the United States.
Jeff Beck drew heavily on blues traditions in his guitar work, both with the Yardbirds and in his solo career.
Mance Lipscomb was a Texas songster who played a wide range of folk, blues, and country songs with intricate fingerpicking.
B.B. King died in Las Vegas at the age of 89.
Boyd Gilmore was a lesser-known Delta blues musician who recorded only a handful of sides in the 1950s.
The wailing vocal style in blues singing mimics the sound of human crying and mourning, giving it its emotional depth.
The 'train' motif is one of the most recurring themes in blues lyrics, symbolizing travel, escape, and longing.
Ana Popovic is a Serbian-born blues-rock guitarist who has become a prominent figure on the modern international blues scene.
Big Walter Horton was a Memphis and Chicago blues harmonica player whose tone was admired by musicians worldwide.
The Jimi Hendrix Experience drew heavily from the blues, with songs like 'Red House' being straight blues numbers.
Samantha Fish is a modern blues guitarist and singer from Kansas City, Missouri, known for her energetic live shows.
The term 'hokum blues' describes a humorous, lighthearted style of blues popular in the 1920s and 1930s.
Eddie 'Son' House was rediscovered by blues enthusiasts in Rochester, New York, in 1964 after years of obscurity.
Blind Lemon Jefferson died in Chicago under mysterious circumstances.
The electric guitar amplifier was essential to the development of Chicago blues, allowing the guitar to be heard over a full band.
Junior Kimbrough was a Mississippi Hill Country blues musician known for his droning, hypnotic guitar style.
Savoy Brown was a British blues band formed in London in 1965 that became part of the British blues boom.
Lurrie Bell is a Chicago blues guitarist and son of harmonica legend Carey Bell.
Blues songs are typically in 4/4 time, though variations like 6/8 time occur in slow blues ballads.
Alexis Korner and Cyril Davies opened the Ealing Blues Club in London in 1962, helping to launch the British blues movement.
The Vocalion label recorded Robert Johnson's sessions in 1936 and 1937 in Texas.
Hadda Brooks was a pianist and singer known as the 'Queen of the Boogie.'
The fife and drum blues tradition of northern Mississippi is one of the oldest surviving forms of African American music.
Muddy Waters died in Westmont, Illinois.
Christone 'Kingfish' Ingram is a young blues guitarist from Clarksdale, Mississippi, who has been hailed as the future of the blues.
The flatted third and flatted seventh intervals are the most defining characteristics of the blues scale.
Victoria Spivey was a classic blues singer and guitarist who ran her own record label, Spivey Records, in the 1960s.
Tampa Red's real name was Hudson Woodbridge, and he was one of the most recorded blues artists of the 1930s and 1940s.
Catfish Blues' by Robert Petway was the basis for Muddy Waters' 'Rollin' Stone.'
The harmonica can play in multiple 'positions,' each offering a different scale and feel suited to different blues keys.
Joe Bonamassa is one of the best-selling modern blues-rock guitarists, having released over 40 albums.
The 'field holler,' a vocal style used by enslaved workers, is considered a direct precursor to the blues.
Drink Small is a South Carolina blues guitarist and singer known as the 'Blues Doctor.'
Son House was born in Riverton, Mississippi.
Roosevelt Sykes was a St. Louis blues pianist known as 'The Honeydripper.'
The Delta blues revival of the 1960s helped forgotten musicians like Mississippi John Hurt and Skip James return to performing.
Jimmy McCracklin was an Oakland-based blues pianist and vocalist who bridged West Coast blues and R&B.
The Hammond B3 organ became a staple in certain blues and R&B settings beginning in the 1960s.
Eddy Clearwater was a Chicago blues guitarist known for his flamboyant stage attire, including Native American headdresses.
The phrase 'paying your dues' originated from the demanding life of touring blues musicians.
Grady Champion is a modern Mississippi blues harmonica player and vocalist.
The I-IV-V chord progression used in blues corresponds to the first, fourth, and fifth notes of a major scale.
Luther 'Guitar Junior' Johnson was a Chicago blues guitarist who performed with Muddy Waters for over a decade.
John Lee Hooker died in his sleep in Los Altos, California.
The electric guitar pickup, first commercially available in the 1930s, transformed the blues by amplifying the guitar's sound.
Jimmy Rogers was Muddy Waters' longtime guitar partner and co-architect of the Chicago blues band sound.
Phillip Walker's 'Hello My Love' became a regional hit in the West Coast blues scene.
The upright or double bass was the original bass instrument in early blues and jazz ensembles.
Louisiana swamp blues is characterized by a slow, humid groove with a reverb-laden guitar sound.
Joe Louis Walker has won multiple Blues Music Awards and is known for his powerful vocals and versatile guitar style.
The blues song 'Crossroads' by Robert Johnson was famously covered by Cream in 1968.
Leo Welch, from Bruce, Mississippi, released his debut album at the age of 81.
Alligator Records, founded by Bruce Iglauer in Chicago in 1971, is one of the leading independent blues labels.
Elmore James died in Chicago of a heart attack.
Floyd Council was a Piedmont blues musician from North Carolina whose nickname inspired the name of the band Pink Floyd.
Barrelhouse piano is a rough, percussive piano style associated with blues played in barrelhouses — informal bars named for storing barrels of alcohol.
Kenny Neal is a Baton Rouge blues musician from a family of blues artists, known for blending swamp blues with zydeco.
The 'moaning' vocal technique in blues singing conveys deep emotion and has roots in African American worship traditions.
Walter 'Wolfman' Washington was a New Orleans blues and funk guitarist known for his smooth style.
The term 'gutbucket' referred to a rough, lowdown style of blues music and to a homemade bass made from a washtub.
Alvin Youngblood Hart is a modern musician who plays country-blues guitar in the acoustic Delta tradition.
The Telecaster, Fender's first solid-body electric guitar, has been used by blues musicians since the 1950s, notably by Muddy Waters.
Marquise Knox is one of the youngest traditional blues artists, starting his performing career as a teenager in St. Louis.
Little Walter died in Chicago after being involved in a street fight.
The 'country blues' label describes the acoustic, rural style of blues that preceded the electrified urban blues.
Stevie Ray Vaughan's band was called Double Trouble, named after an Otis Rush blues song.
Johnnie Bassett was a Detroit blues guitarist whose recordings spanned from the 1950s to the 2000s.
Finger vibrato, a technique where a guitarist rapidly shakes their fretting finger, is used to add expression and sustain in blues.
The shuffle beat in blues drumming accents the second and fourth beats of each measure, driving the groove.
B.B. King's album 'Live at the Regal,' recorded in 1964 at the Regal Theater in Chicago, is considered one of the greatest live blues albums.
R.L. Burnside's music gained new audiences in the 1990s through collaborations with the Jon Spencer Blues Explosion.
The quill, a panpipe-like instrument, was used in early Mississippi blues before the harmonica became dominant.
Omar Kent Dykes was an Austin, Texas, blues musician who fronted the band Omar and the Howlers.
T-Bone Walker was born Aaron Thibeaux Walker in Linden, Texas.
The phrase 'the thrill is gone,' popularized by B.B. King's 1969 hit, originally came from a 1951 Roy Hawkins song.
Magic Slim, born Morris Holt, was a Chicago blues guitarist known for decades of tireless performing on the club circuit.
The 'Dust My Broom' riff, played by Elmore James, is one of the most recognizable slide guitar phrases in blues history.
John Hammond Jr. is a white blues musician who has recorded and performed acoustic blues since the early 1960s.
The minor blues uses minor chords instead of dominant seventh chords for a darker, moodier sound.
The annual Chicago Blues Festival, held in Grant Park, is the largest free blues festival in the world.
Lefty Dizz was a Chicago blues guitarist known for his theatrical stage antics and unconventional playing.
The jump blues of the 1940s, played by artists like Louis Jordan, was an up-tempo, horn-driven precursor to rock and roll.
Joe Turner's record 'Shake, Rattle and Roll' was a number one R&B hit.
Albert King died in Memphis, Tennessee.
The clawhammer banjo technique has similarities to the rhythmic thumb picking used in some Piedmont blues guitar styles.
The term 'rocking the blues' in the 1940s gradually evolved into the term 'rock and roll.'
Smokin' Joe Kubek was a Texas blues guitarist who performed and recorded with B'nois King.
Blues piano players often use 'crushing' — striking two adjacent notes simultaneously — to simulate the effect of a blue note.
The Beale Street Historic District in Memphis, Tennessee, is one of the most iconic streets in blues history.
The Rock and Roll Hall of Fame has inducted numerous blues artists, recognizing the genre as a foundation of rock music.
Carey Bell moved to Chicago from Macon, Mississippi, in 1956 to pursue his blues harmonica career.
The 'fox chase' is a harmonica technique that imitates the sounds of a fox hunt, using rhythmic breathing and tongue effects.
Eddie Taylor was a Chicago blues guitarist who frequently accompanied Jimmy Reed.
Koko Taylor was born Cora Walton near Memphis, Tennessee.

James Cotton was a blues harmonica legend who learned from Sonny Boy Williamson II and played in Muddy Waters' band.

North Mississippi Allstars is a modern band that draws on the Hill Country blues tradition of northern Mississippi.
Sonny Terry was born Saunders Terrell in Greensboro, North Carolina.
Sonny Terry and Brownie McGhee were one of the most famous blues duos, performing together for decades.
The term 'twelve-bar blues' refers to the number of measures in one complete cycle of the chord progression, not the number of bars in a song.
Clarence 'Gatemouth' Brown won the Grammy Award for Best Traditional Blues Recording in 1983 for his album 'Alright Again!'
Walter Vinson was a member of the Mississippi Sheiks and a skilled blues guitarist and mandolinist.

Frank Frost was an Arkansas blues harmonica player, guitarist, and vocalist who recorded for Sun Records and Sam Phillips.
The concept of the 'blues scale' was codified by music theorists, though blues musicians have always played it intuitively.
Drink Small was still performing regularly past the age of 80, earning his reputation as the 'Blues Doctor' of South Carolina.
Buddy Guy was inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame.
Super Chikan is a Hill Country blues musician from Clarksdale, Mississippi, who builds his own guitars from found objects.
The 'stop time' technique, where the band pauses and the vocalist or soloist continues alone, is a dramatic device used in blues.
Charlie Musselwhite is a blues harmonica player who began his career in Chicago in the 1960s and has been active for over 50 years.
The piano blues tradition includes styles as diverse as boogie-woogie, barrelhouse, and sophisticated jazz-blues.
Eddie 'Cleanhead' Vinson was a Texas blues and jazz saxophonist and vocalist known for his bald head and humorous stage banter.
The 'false ending' in blues performance, where a song seems to end but then resumes, is a common showmanship technique.
Rory Gallagher was an Irish blues-rock guitarist whose battered 1961 Fender Stratocaster became iconic.
Beale Street in Memphis has been designated a National Historic Landmark.
Little Feat's music combined blues, rock, funk, and country, led by the slide guitar of Lowell George.
Skip James died in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania.
Lucky Peterson was a blues and R&B multi-instrumentalist who made his television debut at age five on 'The Ed Sullivan Show.'

The Staple Singers blended gospel with blues and soul, making them one of the most important crossover groups of the civil rights era.
Hubert Sumlin was the guitarist for Howlin' Wolf's band and his jagged, inventive style influenced Keith Richards and Jimi Hendrix.
Guitar Slim would walk out into the audience while performing, using an extra-long guitar cable that stretched up to 350 feet.
The minor pentatonic scale, containing five notes — root, flat third, fourth, fifth, and flat seventh — is the most commonly used scale in blues soloing.
Big Joe Williams played a unique nine-string guitar of his own design.

Roosevelt 'Booba' Barnes was a Greenville, Mississippi, juke-joint blues performer known for his raw energy.

Shakey Jake Harris was a Chicago blues harmonica player and uncle to Magic Sam.
The ragtime-influenced Piedmont blues used an alternating thumb bass that mimicked a piano's left hand.
Willie Mae 'Big Mama' Thornton played harmonica and drums in addition to singing.
Geeshie Wiley is one of the most mysterious early blues artists, with only six known recordings from 1930 and 1931.
The 'slow blues' or 'slow drag' is a tempo style typically played at 60 beats per minute or slower, emphasizing emotion.
Selby Flats near Dockery Farms in Mississippi was a gathering point for early Delta blues musicians.
Dockery Farms plantation in the Mississippi Delta is considered a cradle of the blues, where Charley Patton lived and played.
Roy Buchanan was a blues-rock guitarist known for his use of harmonics and the Fender Telecaster.
The term 'piedmont' refers to the foot of the mountain region stretching from Virginia to Georgia where the style developed.

Denise LaSalle was a blues and soul singer-songwriter from Mississippi known for her witty, empowering lyrics.

Henry Townsend was a St. Louis blues musician who recorded from the late 1920s until his death in 2006.
Bettye LaVette is a soul-blues vocalist from Detroit whose career has spanned over six decades.
The New Orleans blues style incorporates second-line rhythms, piano-driven arrangements, and Caribbean influences.
Guitar Shorty is a blues-rock guitarist known for his acrobatic stage performances including backflips while playing.
Hezekiah Early was a St. Louis blues bassist and bandleader active in the 1940s and 1950s.
The 'blue yodel' was a technique associated with Jimmie Rodgers, blending blues vocal inflections with country yodeling.

Big Mama Thornton's original version of 'Hound Dog' featured a rawer, grittier arrangement than Elvis Presley's later pop version.
Memphis Slim was a blues pianist and songwriter from Memphis who became an expatriate in Paris, France, in the 1960s.
The phrase 'woke up this morning' is one of the most common opening lines in blues songs, establishing a first-person narrative.
